sql4stats
  • Informational Resources
    • SQL:Indexing and B-Trees
    • Joins
    • File Formats and DuckDB
  • Theory in Practice
    • DuckDB Native vs. Direct Querying
    • Parquet Querying Time Exploration
    • Join Algorithms and Join Order
  • Tutorials
    • Pivoting + Crosstabs
    • Data Upload Guide

  • References

On this page

  • Why Should a Statistician Care About SQL Databases?
  • Part 1: Getting Data Into a Database
  • Part 2: What Makes a Relational Database “Relational” — Keys

SQL Upload Guide

Building and Keying a SQL Database, Worked Through a SNL Dataset

Why Should a Statistician Care About SQL Databases?

If you do statistics or data science, your default instinct when handed a pile of CSVs is probably to load each one into R or Python and start joining data frames in memory. Working with separate files, however, may lead to issues including files that are too big to fit comfortably in RAM or re-running the same read_csv() calls every session.

A relational database solves these problems by moving the structure of your data out of your analysis script and into the data itself. Instead of a folder of CSVs that only mean something in light of whatever script last touched them, you get a single, queryable system where the relationships between tables, the rules about what counts as valid data, and the logic for combining tables all live in one place.

Concretely, that gets you:

Speed and memory efficiency at scale. A database engine reads only the columns and rows a query actually needs, rather than loading entire files into memory first.

A single source of truth. Once data lives in a database, every person and every script that connects to it sees the same version.

Enforced data integrity. A database can refuse to let you insert nonsense: a row that references an actor who doesn’t exist, a duplicate ID where uniqueness is required. As described below, keys are the mechanism that makes the enforcement possible, and they’re worth understanding even if you are not the person creating the SQL database.

Reusable, composable queries. SQL’s JOIN machinery lets you combine tables on demand, rather than writing bespoke merge logic every time you need a different combination of variables.

We’ll walk through all of this concretely, using a real dataset: every cast member, host, sketch, and impression from Saturday Night Live, scraped from a public repository and loaded into a MariaDB server.

Part 1: Getting Data Into a Database

Where the Data Comes From

For this guide, the source data comes from the snldb GitHub repository (Hilleckes and Morris 2017), which contains a Python scraper for SNL cast, episode, sketch, and appearance data. The specifics of this scraper don’t matter much beyond getting the data necessary to walk through the ideas below. What matters is the general pattern: most real-world data starts out as scraped, exported, or otherwise messy flat files, and getting it into a proper database is itself a meaningful step.

pip install -r requirements.txt
./crawl_single_episode.sh   # test run: one episode
./crawl_all.sh               # full crawl: every episode
python convert_json_to_csv.py

After running the Python code, you end up with a folder of .csv files, one per entity type (e.g., actors, episodes, sketches, etc.). A set of flat files is the starting point that most statisticians actually encounter: a pile of CSVs, related to each other in ways that exist only in your mind (or in a README) but not yet enforced anywhere

Connecting R to a SQL Server

The general pattern for connecting to any SQL database from R is the same regardless of which database engine (e.g., MySQL, MariaDB, PostgreSQL, SQL Server) sits on the other end: you load a driver package, supply connection credentials, and get back a connection object that every subsequent query uses.

Show the code 
library(DBI)
library(RMariaDB)
library(readr)
library(dplyr)
library(stringr)
library(purrr)
library(janitor)

A practice worth adopting immediately, regardless of what data you’re working with: never write credentials directly into a script. Store them as environment variables in a .Renviron file (which should never be committed to version control), and read them in using Sys.getenv() (Wickham and Bryan 2023).

Show the code 
SQL_USER <- Sys.getenv("SQL_USER")
SQL_PWD  <- Sys.getenv("SQL_PWD")
SQL_HOST <- Sys.getenv("SQL_HOST")
SQL_DB   <- "snl"
Show the code 
Sys.setenv(MySQL_TLS_DISABLE_PEER_VERIFICATION = 1)

con_snl <- DBI::dbConnect(
  RMySQL::MySQL(),
  host = SQL_HOST,
  user = SQL_USER,
  password = SQL_PWD,
  dbname = SQL_DB,
  load_data_local_infile = FALSE
)

DBI::dbListTables(con_snl)

dbListTables() is worth calling immediately after establishing a connection, regardless of context. It’s the fastest way to confirm the connection actually succeeded and to see what already exists on the server before you touch anything.

Locating and Validating Source Files Before Upload

Before uploading anything, it’s worth building the habit of checking that your source files are what you think they are. This step generalizes far beyond SNL data: any time you’re about to bulk-load files into a database, a quick sanity pass catches problems while they’re still cheap to fix.

Show the code 
csv_folder <- "~/Desktop/Summer 2026/snldb/output"

csv_files <- list.files(
  path = csv_folder,
  pattern = "\\.csv$",
  full.names = TRUE
)

csv_files
length(csv_files)

if (length(csv_files) == 0) {
  stop("No CSV files found. Check csv_folder path.")
}
Show the code 
csv_check <- map_dfr(csv_files, function(file_path) {
  df <- read_csv(file_path, show_col_types = FALSE)
  
  tibble(
    file = basename(file_path),
    rows = nrow(df),
    columns = ncol(df)
  )
})

csv_check
# A tibble: 11 × 3
   file             rows columns
   <chr>           <int>   <int>
 1 actors.csv       2306       4
 2 appearances.csv 55355       9
 3 casts.csv         614       8
 4 characters.csv   1099       3
 5 episodes.csv      906       4
 6 hosts.csv         925       2
 7 impressions.csv  4115       3
 8 seasons.csv        46       5
 9 sketches.csv      301       2
10 tenure.csv        154       4
11 titles.csv      13704       7

The script above produces a small summary table — file name, row count, column count — for every file about to be uploaded. It seems like a small thing, but it’s the cheapest possible defense against uploading an empty file, a truncated file, or a file you grabbed from the wrong folder.

From File Names to Table Names

SQL table names have stricter rules than file names: no spaces, no leading numbers, no arbitrary punctuation. Rather than handling an ad hoc for every file, it’s worth writing a small helper function that any future project can reuse:

Show the code 
clean_table_name <- function(file_path) {
  file_path |>
    basename() |>
    str_remove("\\.csv$") |>
    janitor::make_clean_names()
}

janitor::make_clean_names() does the heavy lifting — lowercasing, replacing spaces and punctuation with underscores, and resolving duplicate names — which is exactly the kind of small, boring utility worth keeping in your personal toolkit across projects.

Uploading in Chunks, Not All at Once

There is a temptation, with a clean CSV, to dump the whole thing into a database using a single INSERT statement. In practice, a single dump is often slower and less stable than chunking the upload — especially over a network connection to a remote server, where one enormous statement can often time out or simply get rejected outright. Therefore, the safest way to create a database is to upload data in chunks.

Show the code 
upload_csv_to_sql <- function(file_path) {
  table_name <- clean_table_name(file_path)
  
  message("Reading ", basename(file_path), "...")
  
  df <- readr::read_csv(file_path, show_col_types = FALSE) |>
    janitor::clean_names()
  
  message("Rows in CSV: ", nrow(df))
  message("Columns in CSV: ", ncol(df))
  
  if (nrow(df) == 0) {
    stop("CSV has 0 rows: ", basename(file_path))
  }
  
  message("Uploading as table `", table_name, "`...")
  
  DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, paste0("DROP TABLE IF EXISTS `", table_name, "`;"))
  
  DBI::dbCreateTable(
    conn = con_snl,
    name = table_name,
    fields = df
  )
  
  chunk_size <- 250
  n <- nrow(df)
  starts <- seq(1, n, by = chunk_size)
  
  for (start in starts) {
    end <- min(start + chunk_size - 1, n)
    chunk <- df[start:end, ]
    
    insert_sql <- DBI::sqlAppendTable(
      con_snl,
      table = DBI::Id(table = table_name),
      values = chunk,
      row.names = FALSE
    )
    
    DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, insert_sql)
    
    message("Uploaded rows ", start, " to ", end, " of ", n)
  }
  
  row_count <- DBI::dbGetQuery(
    con_snl,
    paste0("SELECT COUNT(*) AS n_rows FROM `", table_name, "`;")
  )
  
  message("Finished uploading `", table_name, "` with ", row_count$n_rows, " rows.")
  
  tibble::tibble(
    table_name = table_name,
    uploaded_rows = row_count$n_rows
  )
}

A few choices here are worth calling out because they generalize to any upload pipeline, not just this one:

The function drops and recreates the table before uploading, rather than appending to whatever might already be there. This makes the script idempotent: you can re-run it as many times as you want, and you’ll always end up with a clean, current copy of the data, rather than accumulating duplicate rows on every re-run.

The row-count confirmation at the end closes the loop. It is important to verify that the row count of the uploaded tables aligns with the known number of rows of the CSV file, from the earlier validation step.

Show the code 
upload_results <- purrr::map_dfr(csv_files, upload_csv_to_sql)
upload_results

At this point, every CSV is now a SQL table, with column types automatically inferred. Typically types are assigned by SQL reading the first rows and observing each column, making an inference of type, which tends to be vague to avoid errors (ints can be classified as double floating point numbers but not the other way around necessarily).

Part 2: What Makes a Relational Database “Relational” — Keys

Loading data into tables gets you tables in a SQL server, but not yet a SQL relational database in the meaningful sense. What turns a set of unrelated tables into an actual relational database is the presence of keys: the formal mechanism that encodes how tables relate to each other and what counts as a valid row.

This is the part of the process most worth understanding deeply, because the concepts here — primary keys, foreign keys, referential integrity — apply identically whether you’re working with SNL sketches, clinical trial data, survey panels, or financial transactions.

Two Types of Keys in SQL

A primary key is a column (or combination of columns) that uniquely identifies every row in a single table, with no duplicates and no missing values allowed.

A foreign key is a column in one table that refers to the primary key of another table. This is what makes joins between tables meaningful rather than coincidental.

For more information of keys along with indexing in SQL see the Informational Resource on Relational aspect of SQL and Keys.

Inspecting the Database Before Changing Anything

Before assigning any keys, the first move with any unfamiliar (or freshly uploaded) database is to look at what’s actually there.

Show the code 
DBI::dbListTables(con_snl)
 [1] "actors"      "appearances" "casts"       "characters"  "episodes"   
 [6] "hosts"       "impressions" "seasons"     "sketches"    "tenure"     
[11] "titles"
Show the code 
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "DESCRIBE actors;")
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "DESCRIBE appearances;")
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "DESCRIBE casts;")
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "DESCRIBE characters;")
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "DESCRIBE episodes;")
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "DESCRIBE hosts;")
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "DESCRIBE impressions;")
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "DESCRIBE seasons;")
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "DESCRIBE sketches;")
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "DESCRIBE tenure;")
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "DESCRIBE titles;")

DESCRIBE shows you each table’s columns, their current data types, whether they accept missing values, and any keys already defined. DESCRIBE is the equivalent of running str() or glimpse() on a data frame in R. This is the first thing you should do before drawing any conclusions about the data’s structure. Consider the following code tha describes (pun intended) the actors table:

DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "DESCRIBE actors;")
   Field         Type Null Key Default Extra
1    aid varchar(255)   NO PRI    <NA>      
2    url         text  YES        <NA>      
3   type         text  YES        <NA>      
4 gender         text  YES        <NA>

The Three Requirements for a Valid Primary Key

For a column to serve as a primary key, it must satisfy two conditions and ideally should meet an additional third condition:

  1. No missing values. A key that’s sometimes absent can’t reliably identify every row.
  2. Every value is unique. If a value repeats, it can’t distinguish between the rows that share it.
  3. It represents a natural identifier for the entity. This one is more judgment-based. Choosing a column that means something as an identifier (an actor’s ID, a patient’s ID, a transaction’s ID) rather than an arbitrary column that happens to be unique by coincidence.

For the SNL data, the natural candidates were the existing ID columns scraped along with the data: actors.aid, characters.charid, episodes.epid, impressions.impid, seasons.sid, sketches.skid, and titles.tid1. This is a common situation where the data often arrives with implicit identifiers already in place, and using them as keys is best practice.

Checking Candidates Before Committing

Rather than trust that an ID column satisfies the three conditions above, it’s worth checking directly. This test of comparing total rows, non-missing values, and distinct values is a general-purpose validity check you can reuse for any candidate key, in any dataset:

Show the code 
check_key <- function(table, col) {
  DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, paste0("
    SELECT 
      '", table, "' AS table_name,
      '", col, "' AS column_name,
      COUNT(*) AS total_rows,
      COUNT(`", col, "`) AS non_missing,
      COUNT(DISTINCT `", col, "`) AS distinct_values,
      SUM(`", col, "` IS NULL) AS missing_values
    FROM `", table, "`;
  "))
}

primary_key_checks <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  check_key("actors", "aid"),
  check_key("characters", "charid"),
  check_key("episodes", "epid"),
  check_key("impressions", "impid"),
  check_key("seasons", "sid"),
  check_key("sketches", "skid"),
  check_key("titles", "tid")
)

primary_key_checks

If total_rows, non_missing, and distinct_values are all equal, and missing_values is zero, the column passes the test. If any of those don’t line up, that’s valuable information for the user to have before committing to a key. That is, any column that fails the test should not become a primary key.

Identifying Foreign Key Relationships

Once primary keys are established, the next question is: which columns in other tables refer back to a primary key? This is fundamentally a modeling question about your data’s structure, and answering it well requires understanding what each table actually represents. Oftentimes, you can identify columns that have the same name across tables.

For the SNL database, the following connections were made: appearances links to actors, titles, characters, impressions, episodes, and seasons (an appearance is the intersection of all of these: a specific actor performing in a specific sketch, in a specific episode); casts links actors to seasons; episodes belongs to a season; hosts links hosts to episodes; titles links to episodes, seasons, and sketches; and tenure links back to actors.

Checking for Orphans Before Enforcing Anything

A foreign key constraint, once created, requires that every value in the child column exists in the parent table. Parent table contains the primary key and child column connects to that primary key. But real-world data is messy, and it’s common to have values that don’t satisfy the parent-child connection, called orphan values. SQL won’t let you create the foreign key constraint until the orphan values are resolved, so it is necessary to find them before trying to make a foreign key that won’t be accepted.

Show the code 
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "
  SELECT c.*
  FROM casts c
  LEFT JOIN episodes e
    ON c.first_epid = e.epid
  WHERE c.first_epid IS NOT NULL
    AND e.epid IS NULL;
")

DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "
  SELECT h.*
  FROM hosts h
  LEFT JOIN actors a
    ON h.aid = a.aid
  WHERE h.aid IS NOT NULL
    AND a.aid IS NULL;
")

This LEFT JOIN ... WHERE parent.key IS NULL pattern directly answers “which rows in my child table don’t have a matching parent?” regardless of what the entities actually are.

In the SNL data, resolving orphan values surfaced two genuine problems: some casts.first_epid values didn’t match any real episode, and one hosts.aid value didn’t match any real actor. Scraped and merged data routinely has small inconsistencies like this, and finding them is the entire point of this check.

Deciding How to Handle Orphans

Once you’ve found orphan values, you have to decide what to do about them. Generally, the options are: correct the value if you can determine what it should have been, delete the row if it’s clearly invalid, or set the offending value to NULL if you can’t confidently do either of the first two options.

Show the code 
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  UPDATE casts c
  LEFT JOIN episodes e
    ON c.first_epid = e.epid
  SET c.first_epid = NULL
  WHERE c.first_epid IS NOT NULL
    AND e.epid IS NULL;
")

DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  UPDATE hosts h
  LEFT JOIN actors a
    ON h.aid = a.aid
  SET h.aid = NULL
  WHERE h.aid IS NOT NULL
    AND a.aid IS NULL;
")

Setting the observed value to NULL here is a deliberately conservative choice: rather than guessing at what the “correct” episode or actor should have been (and risking a false relationship that looks valid but isn’t), the missing-link information is preserved as openly missing.

Verifying the Fix

The same orphan-check pattern from before doubles as a verification step after the fix.

Show the code 
check_fk <- function(child_table, child_col, parent_table, parent_col) {
  DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, paste0("
    SELECT 
      '", child_table, ".", child_col, " -> ", parent_table, ".", parent_col, "' AS relationship,
      COUNT(*) AS orphan_count
    FROM `", child_table, "` c
    LEFT JOIN `", parent_table, "` p
      ON c.`", child_col, "` = p.`", parent_col, "`
    WHERE c.`", child_col, "` IS NOT NULL
      AND p.`", parent_col, "` IS NULL;
  "))
}

fk_checks <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  check_fk("appearances", "aid", "actors", "aid"),
  check_fk("appearances", "tid", "titles", "tid"),
  check_fk("appearances", "charid", "characters", "charid"),
  check_fk("appearances", "impid", "impressions", "impid"),
  check_fk("appearances", "epid", "episodes", "epid"),
  check_fk("appearances", "sid", "seasons", "sid"),
  
  check_fk("casts", "aid", "actors", "aid"),
  check_fk("casts", "sid", "seasons", "sid"),
  check_fk("casts", "first_epid", "episodes", "epid"),
  check_fk("casts", "last_epid", "episodes", "epid"),
  
  check_fk("episodes", "sid", "seasons", "sid"),
  
  check_fk("hosts", "epid", "episodes", "epid"),
  check_fk("hosts", "aid", "actors", "aid"),
  
  check_fk("titles", "epid", "episodes", "epid"),
  check_fk("titles", "skid", "sketches", "skid"),
  check_fk("titles", "sid", "seasons", "sid"),
  
  check_fk("tenure", "aid", "actors", "aid")
)

fk_checks

The test is applied across every foreign key candidate relationship, now we can see if each foreign key relationship is valid.

A Required Technical Prerequisite: Storage Engine and Data Types

Before constraints or foreign keys can actually be created, two technical prerequisites in MySQL/MariaDB specifically need attention.

Storage engine. Foreign keys require a storage engine that supports them: InnoDB, in MySQL/MariaDB’s case. Tables created through bulk CSV import sometimes default to a different engine, so the key storage needs to be checked and corrected:

Show the code 
tables <- DBI::dbListTables(con_snl)

for (table in tables) {
  DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, paste0("ALTER TABLE `", table, "` ENGINE = InnoDB;"))
}

Matching data types. A foreign key constraint requires the child column and the parent column to have compatible types. Since automatic type inference during upload is often loose the column type needs to be specified (e.g., modifying DOUBLE to INT).

Show the code 
# Primary key columns
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE actors MODIFY aid VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE characters MODIFY charid INT NOT NULL;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE episodes MODIFY epid INT NOT NULL;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE impressions MODIFY impid INT NOT NULL;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE seasons MODIFY sid INT NOT NULL;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE sketches MODIFY skid INT NOT NULL;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE titles MODIFY tid INT NOT NULL;")
Show the code 
# appearances
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE appearances MODIFY aid VARCHAR(255);")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE appearances MODIFY tid INT;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE appearances MODIFY charid INT;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE appearances MODIFY impid INT;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE appearances MODIFY epid INT;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE appearances MODIFY sid INT;")

# casts
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE casts MODIFY aid VARCHAR(255);")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE casts MODIFY sid INT;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE casts MODIFY first_epid INT;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE casts MODIFY last_epid INT;")

# episodes
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE episodes MODIFY sid INT;")

# hosts
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE hosts MODIFY epid INT;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE hosts MODIFY aid VARCHAR(255);")

# titles
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE titles MODIFY epid INT;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE titles MODIFY skid INT;")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE titles MODIFY sid INT;")

# tenure
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE tenure MODIFY aid VARCHAR(255);")

Notice that actors.aid is set to be a VARCHAR, not an integer — in the original scraped data, actor IDs are text-based codes rather than plain numbers. Every column referencing it (appearances.aid, casts.aid, hosts.aid, tenure.aid) must match that type exactly. This is a generalizable rule: a foreign key’s type is dictated by whatever the primary key it references actually is.

Creating the Primary Keys

With types corrected and orphans resolved, creating the primary keys themselves is mechanically simple:

Show the code 
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE actors ADD PRIMARY KEY (aid);")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE characters ADD PRIMARY KEY (charid);")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE episodes ADD PRIMARY KEY (epid);")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE impressions ADD PRIMARY KEY (impid);")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE seasons ADD PRIMARY KEY (sid);")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE sketches ADD PRIMARY KEY (skid);")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE titles ADD PRIMARY KEY (tid);")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE casts ADD PRIMARY KEY (aid, sid);")
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "ALTER TABLE tenure ADD PRIMARY KEY (aid);")

Two of the primary key creations in the SNL database deserve a second look, because they illustrate situations that come up regularly in real data modeling.

Composite keys. casts uses (aid, sid) — actor ID and season ID together — as its primary key, rather than any single column. This makes sense once you think about what a row in casts represents: one actor’s membership in one season. Neither aid nor sid alone is unique (an actor appears across many seasons; a season has many actors), but the combination is.

No natural unique identifier at all. Some tables don’t have any column or combination of existing columns that’s guaranteed unique or a meaningful identifier. appearances, in this case, does not have a unique identifier since a single actor could, in principle, appear multiple times in the same sketch in the same episode, with no existing column distinguishing those occurrences. The solution is a surrogate key: a new column, with no real-world meaning, that exists purely to give every row a unique handle.

Show the code 
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE appearances
  ADD COLUMN appearance_id INT NOT NULL AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY FIRST;
")

AUTO_INCREMENT means the database assigns each new row the next sequential integer automatically. Surrogate keys are extremely common in practice; in fact, in many production database designs, every table gets an auto-incrementing surrogate key by default, specifically to sidestep the exact problem of no natural identifier before it ever comes up.

When the “Obvious” Key Doesn’t Quite Work

The original plan for hosts was a composite key of (epid, aid): the same logic as casts. But a primary key, by definition, cannot contain missing values in any of its component columns. So before committing to that plan, it’s worth checking:

Show the code 
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "
  SELECT
    SUM(epid IS NULL) AS missing_epid,
    SUM(aid IS NULL) AS missing_aid
  FROM hosts;
")
Show the code 
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "
  SELECT *
  FROM hosts
  WHERE aid IS NULL;
")

This check found exactly one row with a missing aid, likely a host who isn’t (or wasn’t, at the time of scraping) a cast member with an actor ID on file. Because of that single missing value, (epid, aid) is disqualified as a primary key outright; SQL would refuse to create it. The fix is the same surrogate-key pattern as before:

Show the code 
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE hosts
  ADD COLUMN host_id INT NOT NULL AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY FIRST;
")

A key design that looks correct in theory can be invalidated by a single bad value in practice, and it’s much better to discover that during a deliberate check than to discover it as a cryptic constraint-creation error later. Always verify potential keys before you commit to establishing the keys associated with the database.

Creating the Foreign Keys

With primary keys, type compatibility, and orphan values all resolved, the foreign key constraints themselves can finally be added:

Show the code 
# appearances foreign keys
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE appearances
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_appearances_actor
  FOREIGN KEY (aid) REFERENCES actors(aid);
")

DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE appearances
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_appearances_title
  FOREIGN KEY (tid) REFERENCES titles(tid);
")

DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE appearances
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_appearances_character
  FOREIGN KEY (charid) REFERENCES characters(charid);
")

DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE appearances
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_appearances_impression
  FOREIGN KEY (impid) REFERENCES impressions(impid);
")

DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE appearances
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_appearances_episode
  FOREIGN KEY (epid) REFERENCES episodes(epid);
")

DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE appearances
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_appearances_season
  FOREIGN KEY (sid) REFERENCES seasons(sid);
")

# casts foreign keys
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE casts
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_casts_actor
  FOREIGN KEY (aid) REFERENCES actors(aid);
")

DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE casts
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_casts_season
  FOREIGN KEY (sid) REFERENCES seasons(sid);
")

DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE casts
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_casts_first_episode
  FOREIGN KEY (first_epid) REFERENCES episodes(epid);
")

DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE casts
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_casts_last_episode
  FOREIGN KEY (last_epid) REFERENCES episodes(epid);
")

# episodes foreign key
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE episodes
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_episodes_season
  FOREIGN KEY (sid) REFERENCES seasons(sid);
")

# hosts foreign keys
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE hosts
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_hosts_episode
  FOREIGN KEY (epid) REFERENCES episodes(epid);
")

DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE hosts
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_hosts_actor
  FOREIGN KEY (aid) REFERENCES actors(aid);
")

# titles foreign keys
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE titles
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_titles_episode
  FOREIGN KEY (epid) REFERENCES episodes(epid);
")

DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE titles
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_titles_sketch
  FOREIGN KEY (skid) REFERENCES sketches(skid);
")

DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE titles
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_titles_season
  FOREIGN KEY (sid) REFERENCES seasons(sid);
")

# tenure foreign key
DBI::dbExecute(con_snl, "
  ALTER TABLE tenure
  ADD CONSTRAINT fk_tenure_actor
  FOREIGN KEY (aid) REFERENCES actors(aid);
")

After keys have been established is the moment the database actually becomes “relational” in the formal sense. From this point forward, the database itself, enforces the relationships

Confirming the Final Structure

Finally, it’s worth confirming what was actually built, both as a sanity check and as documentation you (or a collaborator) can return to later.

Show the code 
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "
  SELECT 
    table_name,
    column_name,
    constraint_name,
    referenced_table_name,
    referenced_column_name
  FROM information_schema.key_column_usage
  WHERE table_schema = 'snl'
  ORDER BY table_name, constraint_name, ordinal_position;
")
Show the code 
DBI::dbGetQuery(con_snl, "
  SELECT 
    table_name,
    GROUP_CONCAT(column_name ORDER BY ordinal_position SEPARATOR ', ') AS primary_key
  FROM information_schema.key_column_usage
  WHERE table_schema = 'snl'
    AND constraint_name = 'PRIMARY'
  GROUP BY table_name
  ORDER BY table_name;
")

Querying information_schema on every standard SQL database shows its own structure through this same metadata schema, so the exact query pattern (perhaps with a different table_schemaname) works for inspecting keys in any database you encounter, not just this one.

References

Hilleckes, Hendrik, and Colin Morris. 2017. Snldb: A Saturday Night Live Database. https://github.com/hhllcks/snldb.
Wickham, Hadley, and Jennifer Bryan. 2023. R Packages. 2nd ed. O’Reilly Media. https://r-pkgs.org.

Footnotes

  1. The syntax can represents table.variable where table is the table name and variable is the variable name. actors.aid means the column aid from the table actors.↩︎
